schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch map the chapter's two registers and Ziklag's history; Ellicott presses the obscure clause 'kept himself close' as David shut up or banished from Saul's presence.
Gill fixes the setting: Achish had given Ziklag to David as a safe retreat from Saul's wrath.
Clarke surveys the whole chapter — who joined at Ziklag, the 120,000 at Hebron, the provisions — and notes Ziklag as David's refuge.
Matthew Henry reads providence: while David kept close, God was preparing helpers, for the war was God's and he found out the helpers of it.
Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott render the men ambidextrous archers and slingers, and insist 'Saul's brethren of Benjamin' means his fellow-tribesmen, not near kin.
Gill notes only that they were armed with bows, the weapon of that age.
Wesley names the motive: of Saul's own tribe, yet moved by God's spirit, discerning God's departure from Saul and his presence with David.
Ellicott and Keil trace the names to their towns — Gibeah of Saul, Anathoth, Azmaveth — and note the Korahite doorkeepers among them, the picked men of Saul's own country.
Gill marks Shemaah the Gibeathite of Gibeah, Saul's birthplace, so some of these may have been the king's kindred.
Ellicott makes Ismaiah a Benjamite only by domicile, reckoned among the famous 'thirty' though absent from the catalogue, and reads 'over the thirty' as captain or as 'above the thirty' in prowess.
Gill calls him a mighty man among the thirty who came with a company under his command.
Wesley takes 'the thirty' as thirty valiant Benjamites Ismaiah led and commanded.

debates.question: What is 'the thirty,' and what does 'over the thirty' mean?

sides.position: David's famous corps of heroes, into which Ismaiah was reckoned though unlisted, he being captain over or above them
simply a retinue of thirty Benjamites whom Ismaiah led as their commander

schools.summary: Ellicott mines the names: 'Bealiah' means 'Baal is Jah,' evidence that Baal was once a title of the God of Israel; 'the Haruphite' ties the clan to the sons of Hariph among the returned exiles.
Gill notes Shephatiah the Haruphite as a native of Haruph or Hariph.

Keil takes the Korhites for descendants of the Levite Korah, doorkeepers of the sanctuary whose office may have moved them to seek the anointed; Ellicott leans the same way but allows a Judean clan Korah.
Gill insists they are not Levites at all but the posterity of a Korah of Benjamin.

debates.question: Who are the 'Korhites' of this verse?

sides.position: descendants of the Levite Korah, of priestly stock
posterity of a Korah belonging to Benjamin, not Levites
possibly members of the otherwise-unknown Judean clan Korah

schools.summary: Ellicott weighs why the Judean town Gedor stands in a Benjamite list, preferring shifting town-ownership and mixed border populations to the theory of two welded lists.
Gill supposes Gedor 'might now belong to Benjamin,' or that another city of the same name lay within it.

debates.question: Why does the Judean town-name Gedor appear in a Benjamite roll?

sides.position: the chronicler welded two separate lists into one
towns changed hands and border towns held mixed populations

schools.summary: Ellicott reads the lion-faced, gazelle-swift description as verse betraying an ancient source the chronicler borrowed, comparing David's elegy over Saul and Jonathan.
Gill explains 'separated themselves' — cutting off from tribe, family, and Saul's government to reach David's stronghold.
Clarke notes swiftness was reckoned a grand accomplishment in a warrior throughout antiquity.
Matthew Henry calls their leaving 'a laudable separation' and finds a pattern of venturing in dependence on divine protection.

Ellicott glosses 'the first' as the chief; Keil reads 'one over a hundred, the greatest over a thousand' as the weakest a match for a hundred foes and the strongest a thousand (Lev. 26:8), against the Vulgate.
Gill lists the eleven, ranked by age, merit, or order of coming to David.
Ellicott prefers the margin — the least a match for a hundred, the greatest for a thousand — and calls the poetic notice unlike the chronicler's style; David's band was then about 600.
Gill takes 'captains of the host' as officers of the militia at home and afterward in David's army.
Matthew Henry notes they were officers who, though coming alone, had hundreds and thousands at command.
Ellicott and Keil make the swollen spring flood the point, the Gadites crossing when Jordan overflowed and routing the valley-dwellers east and west.
Clarke dates it to Nisan, before the mountain snows melted, and reads the danger as proof of their heroism.
Gill notes they went over Jordan in the first month to reach David.
Matthew Henry: daring men who broke through the flood, fit for God's cause because they ventured in dependence on divine protection.
Keil supplies the band's chief Amasai (perhaps Amasa) and notes their names were not preserved; Ellicott points back to 'the hold' of v.8.
Gill records men of Benjamin and Judah besides those already named.
Matthew Henry marks their leader Amasai and David's reason for suspicion of the two tribes that had betrayed him.
Keil and Ellicott find in David's speech and Amasai's reply the marks of genuine antiquity, and read 'the God of our fathers behold and punish' as a jussive committing the cause to God.
Gill: David went out from suspicion, to sound men largely of Saul's tribe before admitting them.
Matthew Henry praises David's prudent, fair treaty — rewarding the faithful, leaving the false to the God who says 'Vengeance is mine.'
Wesley: by 'the God of our fathers' David both warns them and encourages himself that God would right him.

Ellicott reads 'spirit clothed Amasai' with 'God' dropped from the text, holds enthusiasm for truth to be a sort of divine possession, identifies Amasai with Amasa, and lays out the oath's poetic structure.
Clarke passes on the Targum's expansion of the oath; Wesley insists heroical and generous motions, not only saving graces, are ascribed to God's Spirit.
Gill calls it a spirit of fortitude clothing Amasai, David's sister's son.
Matthew Henry unfolds the oath clause by clause — 'Thine are we, David'; 'peace to thy helpers'; 'thy God helpeth thee' — and turns it to allegiance to the Son of David.

themes: allegiance to the Son of David

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott set the desertion before Gilboa: David marched out with the Philistines but helped them not, their lords sending him back lest 'he fall to his master Saul' — a summary of 1 Samuel 29.
Gill notes only that some of Manasseh fell to David and took his part.
Matthew Henry sees providence timing the defection, seasonable and mighty men joining as David marched home.
Keil names the seven captains of thousands; Ellicott dates their coming to the eve of Saul's fall, judges the doubled Jozabad a scribal slip, and reads 'thousand' as clan.
Gill marks these off as a second, later Manassite accession on the return to Ziklag.
Wesley sets it as David returned from the Philistine camp to Ziklag.

Ellicott finds the Hebrew obscure and offers 'helped David over the band' — joint command of his forces — beside the usual sense of help against the Amalekite raiders; he treats vv.21-22 as the list's subscription.
Gill: they helped David against the Amalekites who had burnt Ziklag and carried off the women.
Wesley: against the Amalekites whom David and his six hundred were pursuing.

debates.question: What does 'they helped David against the band' mean?

sides.position: against the Amalekite band of rovers that had burnt Ziklag
'over the band' — set over David's own forces in joint command

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott read 'a host of God' as greatness measured by power and spirit, not number, with the antique colouring of Jacob's camp at Mahanaim, and deny any exaggeration.
Gill dates the daily-swelling ranks from Saul's defeat to David's coming to Hebron.
Clarke calls it an army like the host of the angel of God; Wesley, innumerable as the stars or angels, both named God's hosts.
Matthew Henry: David's interest grew when he needed it — leave it to God to bring the promise to birth.

themes: a great host like the host of God

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott read 'numbers of the heads' as bands or totals, not chiefs (no chiefs being named but the Aaronites), and gloss 'turn the kingdom' and 'the word of the LORD' spoken by Samuel.
Gill: bands trained and accounted for war.
Matthew Henry frames the coronation quota per tribe and the rule that the nearest tribes brought the fewest, from prudence not coldness.
Clarke dates the gathering after the death of Ish-bosheth.

themes: according to the word of the LORD

schools.summary: Keil records 6,800 armed men; Ellicott notes the list runs south to north and explains Judah's small number — it had crowned David seven years before and needed not appear in force.
Wesley: they came in the name of their brethren, for the whole tribe stuck to David.
Matthew Henry: the nearest tribes brought fewest from prudence, being within call.
Keil records Simeon's 7,100; Ellicott refines 'mighty men of valour' to men fit for warfare or military service.
Matthew Henry: a near tribe within Judah's territory brought few, ready within call — prudence, not coldness.
Ellicott reads 'the sons of the Levite' as gentilic and defends martial Levites as guardians of the sanctuaries; Keil takes the 4,600 as the tribal sum from which the priestly companies are drawn.

themes: the tribe of Levi in arms

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott make Jehoiada prince of the house of Aaron and head of its warriors — not the high priest, since Abiathar held that office — and question whether the 3,700 are counted within the 4,600.
Clarke and Wesley agree Jehoiada was captain over Aaron's warriors under the high priest Abiathar, not himself the high priest.

Ellicott and Keil identify the young warrior Zadok, likely the future high priest who replaced Abiathar, whose father's house of Eleazar was strong enough to muster twenty-two princes; the Levite total is left unstated.
Keil and Ellicott explain Benjamin's meagre 3,000: most still kept the ward of Saul's house, their reluctance to own David surviving even the murder of Ish-bosheth.
Wesley: they had endeavoured to keep the crown in Saul's family.
Matthew Henry: a foolish tribal affection held them back — kindred should never over-rule conscience.

Keil reads the 20,800 as 'men of name,' renowned by their fathers' houses; Ellicott finds their comparative smallness noticeable, guessing decline since Moses or losses in Saul's last Philistine wars.
Keil and Ellicott read 'appointed by name' as a formal tribal consultation and enrolment — 18,000 of western Manasseh levied and listed by their chiefs to make David king.
Wesley: men not ashamed publicly to own David by putting their names to the roll.

Ellicott and Keil read 'understanding of the times' as political sagacity, not astrology, referring it to the two hundred heads, not the whole tribe, and reporting yet rejecting the older astronomical view.
Clarke preserves the Targum's picture of Issachar as astronomers and calendar-makers; Wesley reads political discernment, timing their allegiance to Abner's death.
Matthew Henry allows natural, ceremonial, or political 'times,' but leans to statecraft — 'it is the periphrasis of statesmen that they know the times.'

debates.question: What was Issachar's 'understanding of the times'?

sides.position: political and statesmanlike sagacity, discerning the hour to make David king
literal astronomical, calendrical, even astrological skill

themes: the men who knew the times
two hundred heads leading the tribe

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott render 'keep rank' as closing up the battle array (against the LXX/Vulgate 'help') and 'not of a double heart' as undivided fidelity, defending the large number against doubt.
Wesley: sincerely loyal, not dissembling, none with separate interests but all for the public good.
Matthew Henry: Israelites indeed, in whom was no guile.
Keil records a thousand captains and 37,000 men, reckoned by officers then rank and file; Ellicott notes the spear here is a different, perhaps thrown, weapon from Judah's thrusting-spear.
Matthew Henry's rule that the remote tribes measured their zeal by the size of the host they marched so far to bring.

Keil records Dan's 28,600 expert in war; Ellicott notes the gentilic 'the Danite' and stresses that Dan — often omitted in the chronicler's genealogies — is here included, a mark of the completeness of Israel's consent.
Ellicott glosses 'expert in war' as marshalling the battle-line ('rangers of battle'); Keil records Asher's 40,000 from the far north-west.
Matthew Henry: a remote tribe bringing many, its devotion measured by the multitude it marched down the length of the land.
Keil and Ellicott record 120,000 from beyond Jordan; Ellicott calls the total remarkable yet declines to emend it for want of any parallel document to check the ciphers.
Wesley marks this as the eastern half of Manasseh, distinct from the western half already numbered.
Matthew Henry: the farthest tribes brought the most, their zeal measured by their multitude and their march.

Ellicott and Keil join 'keep rank' and 'perfect heart,' read 'the rest' as those absent from Hebron so that all Israel was unanimous, and reckon the assembly above 300,000, man and warrior being convertible terms.
Gill: well-disciplined men who could keep rank.
Matthew Henry makes the perfect, single heart the day's glory — Israelites without guile, none with separate interests, all for the public good.
Keil and Delitzsch describe the three days' festive meals, the nearer tribes providing for the rest.
Gill: a festival to testify their joy on the occasion.
Clarke cautions that only the deputies feasted, and breaks into praise of David as unrivalled statesman, warrior, poet, and divine, worthy of the public choice.
Matthew Henry: joy in Israel, for when the righteous rule the city rejoices — a figure of the joy when Christ's throne is set up in the soul.

Keil and Ellicott spread out the provisions brought from near and far — figs, raisin-cakes, wine, oil, on asses, camels, mules, oxen but not horses — and hear in the simple diction, 'reminding us of Homer's feasts,' a mark of the record's antiquity.
Gill: those nearby, bordering on Judah, brought supplies.
Matthew Henry: joy in Israel, for when the righteous bear rule the city rejoices, with provision made not for days but for the whole life and for eternity.
